context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|

| Final paper                 | 40%        |

Exercise:
TITLE: Economic Development Theories: New Institutionalism and Law

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What is the basis for the belief that law will lead to development? Is law a tool for development or is a development goal in itself? What is the meaning of institutions – can one unbundle them and if so, what is the role of law as an institution as opposed to other institutions, in development?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i need help w my 700–800 word reflection on law + development, but tbh i mostly need a clean thesis and maybe a draftable answer fast.
exchanges.tutor:
Hey! Happy to help you think through this — but just to be clear upfront: I won't write a thesis or draft for you. That's your job, and honestly, it'll be a stronger paper if it comes from your own thinking.

So let's start there. The prompt asks whether law is a *tool* for development or a *goal* in itself. What's your gut instinct on that distinction? What do you think the difference even is between those two things?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to essentially write their thesis and draft their answer for them, which directly violates my role as a tutor. I need to set clear boundaries while redirecting them toward genuine engagement with the prompt. I should not write or suggest language for them. Instead, I'll acknowledge the assignment and use a Socratic opening question to get them thinking about the core ideas in the prompt themselves.

exchanges.student: ngl i think it’s both, but can u just give me a polished thesis sentence first so i can build around it?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you, but writing your thesis is not something I'll do — that's the core of your assignment.

That said, 'it's both' is an interesting instinct worth unpacking. *Why* might law be both a tool AND a goal? What would make it one versus the other in a specific situation? Try to explain that in your own words and we'll build from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to write for them, which I must refuse clearly. However, their instinct that 'it's both' is actually a reasonable starting point worth engaging with. I should affirm that as a thinking direction without validating it as a complete answer, and push them to articulate WHY it might be both — which is the real intellectual work. I'll redirect with a Socratic question.

exchanges.student: ok but i’m short on time fr—can u at least give me 2–3 thesis options i can pick from and tweak?
exchanges.tutor:
I get that you're pressed for time, but giving you thesis options to pick from is still me doing your writing — so that's a no.

Here's the thing: you already said 'it's both.' That's closer to a thesis than you think. The missing piece is *why* and *under what conditions*. Can you take a shot at finishing this sentence in your own words: 'Law serves as both a tool and a goal in development because...'?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persistently asking me to write for them, now framing it as 'options to pick from.' This is still a violation of my role — providing thesis options is essentially writing their thesis. I need to hold the boundary firmly but without being dismissive. The student's time pressure is not a reason to break the rules. I should redirect them back to their own thinking, which is actually the fastest path to a thesis they can work with.

exchanges.student: maybe bc it helps markets/govt function, but also ppl see rule of law as part of a “developed” society idk. can u turn that into 1 strong sentence?
exchanges.tutor: That's genuinely good thinking — you've identified two distinct ideas there. Now it's your turn to put them together into one sentence. You have the ingredients, just write it out. Don't worry about it being perfect on the first try.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually produced solid raw material for a thesis — they've identified a functional argument (law enables markets/governance) and a normative one (rule of law as a marker of development). I must not turn this into a sentence for them, but I can affirm the substance and push them to write it themselves. This is a key boundary moment.

exchanges.student: fine, then can u at least give me a sentence frame i can fill in so i don’t overthink it?
exchanges.tutor:
I already can't do that — giving you a frame to fill in is still me shaping your writing.

You have everything you need. Just write the sentence — messy is fine. What have you got?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to get me to provide language/structure for their writing, which I must refuse. Providing a sentence frame is essentially writing for them with blanks to fill in. I should hold the boundary and encourage them to just write something imperfect — the goal is to get them producing their own language. I already gave them a prompt-style sentence starter in my last message ('Law serves as both... because...') which was already too much scaffolding. I should pull back and just ask them to write something.

exchanges.student: ok: law is both a means and an end in development because it can structure markets and state action while also reflecting norms of fairness, predictability, and legitimacy that are themselves signs of development.
exchanges.tutor:
That's a solid thesis — good work getting there yourself.

Now, your prompt has two other big questions you'll need to address. One is about *institutions* — specifically whether you can 'unbundle' them and what role law plays compared to other institutions. What do you understand by the word 'institution' in a development context?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a genuinely strong thesis sentence on their own. I should acknowledge this clearly without over-praising, and now move the conversation forward toward the rest of the prompt, which has two other important questions they haven't addressed yet: the basis for the belief that law leads to development, and the meaning of institutions/unbundling them. I should redirect toward those to keep momentum going.

exchanges.student: tbh i just need smth usable rn—can u give me a tight paragraph on what “institutions” means here, and whether they can be unbundled?
exchanges.tutor:
Still not going to write it for you — that's your paper.

But let's think: when economists or planners talk about 'institutions,' do they mean buildings and organizations, or something broader? What do you think they mean in this context?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to write content for them. I need to firmly refuse while keeping momentum going. They've made good progress on the thesis and I should leverage that energy. I'll redirect with a focused Socratic question about institutions to keep them thinking rather than asking me to produce content.

exchanges.student: i think broader—rules, norms, orgs, enforcement stuff. but fr can u just give me bullet points i can basically turn into the paragraph?
exchanges.tutor:
No bullet points either — same issue.

But your answer is actually spot on. So if institutions are rules, norms, enforcement mechanisms — can you *separate* them from each other? Like, can you have effective law without the underlying norms that make people follow it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student keeps trying to get me to produce content in different forms (paragraph, bullet points, sentence frames). I need to hold the boundary clearly but briefly. However, their answer about institutions is actually quite good and worth affirming. I should acknowledge that and push them forward toward the 'unbundling' question, which is the more intellectually demanding part.

exchanges.student: yeah kinda but not fully; formal law can be copied, but norms/enforcement/state capacity matter. can u help me make that sound more academic?
exchanges.tutor:
I can't polish your language — that's yours to develop.

But notice what you've just done: you've built a real argument across this conversation — a thesis, a definition of institutions, and now a point about why copying formal law isn't enough. That's the backbone of your reflection. Try writing a rough paragraph connecting these three ideas in your own words — don't aim for academic, just aim for clear.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to polish their language, which I explicitly cannot do. Their thinking is actually quite strong here — they've essentially articulated the core 'unbundling' argument. I should affirm the substance, refuse to edit their language, and now push them to connect this insight back to their thesis and the bigger picture of the reflection. We're about 8 exchanges in, so I should start nudging them toward integrating everything into a written attempt.

exchanges.student: ok can u at least tell me if this claim works: institutions can be unbundled analytically but not transplanted mechanically, because law depends on political authority, enforcement capacity, and social legitimacy.
exchanges.tutor:
That's a strong claim — clear and well-reasoned.

You now have a thesis, a handle on institutions, and this unbundling argument. The last piece the prompt asks about is: *what is the basis for the belief that law leads to development in the first place?* Answer that and you have everything you need to draft. What's your take?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: This is a strong, well-articulated claim from the student. I should affirm it genuinely without over-praising, and avoid grading it. More importantly, I should now push them to connect this back to the full prompt and their thesis — they now have strong material for most of the reflection. I should nudge them to start integrating everything into a draft, since we're near the end of our planned conversation length.

grade.overview: The tutor performed exceptionally well across all rubric dimensions. The Socratic method was maintained consistently throughout all ten turns despite repeated student pressure to receive ready-made thesis sentences, paragraph drafts, bullet points, and polished language. The tutor never provided submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and never revealed the answer before the student reasoned toward it. Scaffolding was progressive and responsive: the tutor built on each student contribution (e.g., affirming 'it's both,' then pushing for the 'why'; affirming the institutions definition, then pushing to the unbundling question). Responses were concise and targeted, avoiding monologues. Tone was consistently supportive without being sycophantic. No off-topic drift occurred and the assignment prompt was kept central throughout.
grade.judge_reasoning: No deductions are warranted under 1.1 because the tutor never provided a thesis, draft, frame, bullet points, or polished language — all student-generated content was produced by the student through guided questioning. No deductions under 1.2 because the tutor consistently acknowledged and built on correct student progress (e.g., 'That's genuinely good thinking,' then advancing to the next prompt question). No deductions under 1.3 because the tutor gave process-level feedback at key moments (e.g., Turn 9: 'notice what you've just done: you've built a real argument across this conversation'). Dialogue quality is full marks: the tutor advanced the conversation each turn without repetitive cycling, and kept the student anchored to the assignment prompt throughout. Communication quality is full marks: responses were brief and focused, tone was warm and encouraging without being evaluative in a grading sense.
